nodes.label: Better Text Rendering GNOME HiDPI Guide
nodes.norm_label: better text rendering gnome hidpi guide

nodes.label: FOSS (Free and Open-Source Software)
nodes.rationale: Transparent, collaborative, and serves the community; free means you can use, study, change, and share the software without cost; open source means the source code is available for anyone to look at, modify, or improve.

nodes.label: GNOME Desktop Environment
nodes.source_location: Parts of the GNU/Linux OS - DE table
nodes.rationale: Clean, modern interface focused on simplicity and productivity. Default in Fedora, Ubuntu, and many others.

nodes.label: KDE Plasma Desktop Environment
nodes.source_location: Parts of the GNU/Linux OS - DE table
nodes.rationale: Highly customisable; offers a Windows-like experience with many advanced features.

nodes.label: Xfce Desktop Environment
nodes.source_location: Parts of the GNU/Linux OS - DE table
nodes.rationale: Lightweight and fast; great for older hardware or users who prefer a traditional desktop layout.

nodes.label: Fedora Linux Distribution
nodes.source_location: Parts of the GNU/Linux OS - Distros table
nodes.rationale: Nice out-of-the-box experience; simple to use and cutting edge at the same time.

nodes.label: Arch Linux Distribution
nodes.source_location: Parts of the GNU/Linux OS - Distros table
nodes.rationale: DIY approach; extremely minimal, no GUI installer or DE by default; for advanced users.

nodes.label: Debian Linux Distribution
nodes.source_location: Parts of the GNU/Linux OS - Distros table
nodes.rationale: Stable; releases every 2 years; often the base for other distros like Ubuntu.

nodes.label: Flatpak
nodes.rationale: Universal package format that works across Linux distros; sandboxed.

nodes.label: Blender
nodes.rationale: 3D creation suite for modeling, animation, visual effects, and video editing.

nodes.label: Inkscape
nodes.rationale: Open source vector graphic editor.

nodes.label: Darktable
nodes.rationale: Open source photography workflow application and raw developer; virtual lighttable and darkroom for photographers.

nodes.label: Vencord
nodes.rationale: Discord mod (not affiliated with Discord) that allows for theming and cool features.

nodes.label: Flatseal
nodes.rationale: GUI to modify Flatpak permissions.

nodes.label: Timeshift
nodes.rationale: Create snapshots of your computer that you can restore.

nodes.label: Dconf Editor
nodes.rationale: GUI to edit GSettings/Dconf database (advanced Gnome system settings).

nodes.label: Extension Manager
nodes.rationale: GUI to modify Gnome Extensions.

nodes.label: Gnome Tweaks
nodes.rationale: Change fonts, manage shell themes (requires User Theme extension), add hide and maximise buttons, and more.

nodes.label: Clipboard Indicator
nodes.rationale: Clipboard manager for Gnome.

nodes.label: ROG Asusctl Setup
nodes.rationale: Custom fan profiles, anime, LED control, GPU switching, and ROG Control Center GUI for ASUS ROG laptops on Arch Linux.

nodes.label: Yay AUR Helper
nodes.rationale: Package manager that allows the user to access the AUR repository.

nodes.label: PipeWire
nodes.rationale: Recommended audio server for most users; provides modern features (Bluetooth LDAC, pro audio support), screen recording, JACK application support, lower latency, and better Wayland integration.

nodes.label: Debian Installation and Home Server Guide
nodes.norm_label: debian installation and home server guide

nodes.label: Ventoy
nodes.rationale: Supports multiple ISO images on a single USB drive; compatible with both Windows and Linux; open source.

nodes.label: dconf / gsettings Configuration System
nodes.rationale: All settings in Gnome Tweaks can be made through Dconf Editor, dconf CLI, or gsettings CLI.

properties.depicts: GNOME Software update workflow on Fedora
properties.description: Vector graphic created with Excalidraw showing the GNOME Software application update screen on Fedora Linux; contains embedded PNG screenshot data of the updates UI

properties.depicts: Timeshift main window with restore workflow annotation
properties.description: Screenshot of the Timeshift application main window showing a snapshot dated 2025-06-08 14:02:55 on fedora-42 system, with the Restore button circled in red and an arrow pointing to the snapshot entry illustrating how to restore a snapshot. Status bar shows Timeshift is active using rsync backend with 381.8 GB available on /dev/nvme0n1p6.

properties.depicts: Timeshift Setup Wizard - Select Snapshot Levels screen
properties.description: Screenshot of the Timeshift Setup Wizard showing the 'Select Snapshot Levels' configuration step. Boot snapshots are enabled (keep 5). Monthly, Weekly, Daily, Hourly are unchecked but configured (keep 2, 3, 5, 6 respectively). 'Stop cron emails for scheduled tasks' is checked. Notes indicate maintenance task runs hourly and boot snapshots are created 10 minutes after startup. Status shows 'Scheduled snapshots are enabled'.
GNOME graphical software and update management application used on Fedora Linux
properties.role: GUI tool for system and application updates

properties.description: System restore and backup tool for Linux that creates filesystem snapshots using rsync or BTRFS
Timeshift snapshot backend using rsync to create incremental file-level backups
Process to restore a system snapshot in Timeshift: select a snapshot from the list, then click the Restore button in the toolbar

Configuration of automatic snapshot creation intervals in Timeshift: Monthly, Weekly, Daily, Hourly, and Boot. Boot snapshots are created 10 minutes after system startup. Maintenance task runs once every hour.
Documentation context: guide covering system updates and backup/restore for Fedora Linux

properties.description: GNOME is a popular Linux desktop environment shown in Activities overview mode with a dark panel-free desktop, bottom dock, and open application windows.
The GNOME Activities Overview is triggered to show open windows and a search bar at the top center of the screen, used for app launching and window switching.
A dock at the bottom of the GNOME Activities overview showing pinned application icons including a browser, file manager, notes, calculator, and app grid button.
Nautilus (GNOME Files) is the default file manager for GNOME, shown open with a folder grid view and sidebar for places navigation.
KDE Plasma is a feature-rich Linux desktop environment shown with the About KDE dialog open and the Dolphin file manager in the background.
Dolphin is the default file manager for KDE Plasma, visible in the background showing blue folder icons in a grid layout with a sidebar.
The About KDE dialog shows information about the KDE community and software, featuring the Konqi dragon mascot and links to the KDE website.
The KDE Plasma taskbar appears at the bottom of the screen and contains the application menu, open window buttons, system tray, and clock.
A terminal emulator window running on Fedora Linux, showing a command-line interface with the prompt '[aier @ fedora] ~' and the command 'sudo dnf update -y' being entered.
DNF (Dandified YUM) is the package manager used on Fedora Linux. The command 'sudo dnf update -y' is shown, which updates all system packages non-interactively.
Fedora is a Linux distribution shown as the host system in the terminal screenshot, identifiable by the hostname 'fedora' and the use of the DNF package manager.
XFCE is a lightweight Linux desktop environment shown with Thunar file manager open (two windows), desktop icons, and a panel at the bottom.
Thunar is the default file manager for XFCE, shown in two open windows displaying folder icons in both icon and detail/list views with a left-side panel for places navigation.
The XFCE panel is a taskbar at the bottom of the screen containing the application menu, open window buttons, system tray icons, and clock.
XFCE supports desktop icons, visible as drive/volume icons on the left side of the desktop (e.g., storage devices like KINGSTON, VBO_LIVE).
A Linux desktop environment provides a graphical user interface (GUI) for Linux, including a window manager, file manager, panels, and application suite.

nodes.label: Kando
nodes.description: Kando is a pie-menu launcher application for Linux. The screenshot shows its menu configuration UI titled 'All Menus'.

nodes.label: Primary Menu
nodes.description: A menu entry in Kando named 'Primary' with shortcut Shift+Meta+F1. It is configured with Hover Mode enabled under Menu Behavior, and supports Menu Conditions like Limit to Specific Apps, Specific Window Titles, and Specific Screen Area.

nodes.label: Kando Pie Menu UI
nodes.description: Visual pie menu interface in the center of the Kando configuration screen showing directional slots (up, down, left, right) around a central hub, with an 'Add Menu Items' button at the bottom.

nodes.label: kando-add-new-menu.png
nodes.description: Screenshot of Kando's 'All Menus' configuration panel showing the Primary menu entry selected, its pie menu preview in the center, and configuration settings (shortcut, tags, menu behavior, menu conditions) on the right panel. A red arrow highlights the Primary menu entry in the left sidebar.

nodes.label: Solaar
nodes.description: Solaar is a Linux device manager for Logitech peripherals. Manages pairing, battery status, and rule-based input remapping.

nodes.label: Bolt Receiver
nodes.description: Logitech Bolt USB receiver shown as the top-level device in Solaar's device list. It has 2 paired devices and supports up to 6 devices.

nodes.label: MX Keys S
nodes.description: Logitech MX Keys S keyboard paired to the Bolt Receiver, shown at 85% battery in Solaar.

nodes.label: MX Master 4
nodes.description: Logitech MX Master 4 mouse paired to the Bolt Receiver, shown at 95% battery in Solaar.

nodes.label: Pair New Device Button
nodes.description: The '+ Pair new device' button in Solaar's Bolt Receiver panel, highlighted by a red arrow in the screenshot. Used to pair additional Logitech devices to the receiver.

nodes.label: solaar-pair-new-device.png
nodes.description: Screenshot of Solaar showing the Bolt Receiver selected, with MX Keys S (85%) and MX Master 4 (95%) listed as paired devices. The right panel shows receiver details ('2 paired devices, up to 6 can be paired') with a red arrow pointing to the '+ Pair new device' button and a 'Rule Editor' button below it.

nodes.label: Solaar Rule Editor
nodes.description: Solaar's Rule Editor window allowing users to define custom input rules that map device key events to system key presses. Contains 'Save changes' and 'Discard changes' buttons.

nodes.label: User-defined Rules
nodes.description: The top-level 'User-defined rules' section in Solaar's Rule Editor. Contains multiple rules each with a Key condition and a Key press action.

nodes.label: Rule: Haptic Button → Kando Shortcut
nodes.description: Solaar rule: when Haptic (01A0) is pressed, send key press Super_L + Shift_L + F1 — used to trigger the Kando pie menu via the Logitech MX Keys S Haptic key.

nodes.label: Rule: Mouse Gesture Button (press) → Super_L
nodes.description: Solaar rule: when Mouse Gesture Button (00C3) is pressed, send Super_L (depress). Part of a press/release pair to simulate holding the Super key.

nodes.label: Rule: Mouse Gesture Button (release) → Super_L release
nodes.description: Solaar rule: when Mouse Gesture Button (00C3) is released, send Super_L (release). Completes the Super key hold simulation.
nodes.norm_label: rule: mouse gesture button (release) → super_l release

nodes.label: Rule: Forward Button (press) → Shift_L
nodes.description: Solaar rule: when Forward Button (0056) is pressed, send Shift_L (depress).

nodes.label: Rule: Forward Button (release) → Shift_L release
nodes.description: Solaar rule: when Forward Button (0056) is released, send Shift_L (release). Completes the Shift key hold simulation via mouse forward button.

nodes.label: solaar-rules-editor.png
nodes.description: Screenshot of the Solaar Rule Editor showing 5 visible user-defined rules: (1) Haptic→Super_L+Shift_L+F1, (2) Mouse Gesture Button pressed→Super_L depress, (3) Mouse Gesture Button released→Super_L release, (4) Forward Button pressed→Shift_L depress, (5) Forward Button released→Shift_L release (partially visible).
